literal: ultimate-meaning truth; from parama 'highest' + artha 'meaning, object, purpose' + satya 'truth'
invariant_definition: *Paramārtha-satya* is the ultimate-truth member of the Buddhist two-truths distinction. It does not mean a second world hidden behind the conventional one. It names the truth disclosed when analysis no longer takes conventional designations and dependently arisen phenomena as bearing *svabhāva*. In Mādhyamaka this means the emptiness of intrinsic nature; in Yogācāra the term is drawn toward the perfected nature disclosed when subject-object construction is removed. The apparatus is therefore epistemic, ontological, and pedagogical at once: one teaches and practices within *saṃvṛti*, but liberation requires seeing what conventional designation conceals. Comparison with Advaita's *pāramārthika* can be illuminating, but the terms must not be collapsed. Advaita populates the highest level with *brahman*; Buddhist uses of *paramārtha* are precisely designed to block reification at that level.

per_school.definition: For Gauḍapāda, duality is māyāmātra and advaita alone is true paramārthataḥ [cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.17]. In Śaṅkara's Gauḍapādīya reception, paramārthatā is the standpoint from which origination, bondage, striving, and liberation are all denied as ultimately applicable [cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32].

The Sāṅkhya commentary tradition uses paramārthatayā to distinguish the puruṣa's true status from the apparent agency generated by conjunction with the guṇas: the guṇas act, while puruṣa is in truth akartṛ [cite://isvara-krsna/sankhya-karika/skmv_20]. This is not a satyadvaya doctrine, but a paramārtha-marking contrast between the non-agent witness and the active guṇa-complex [cite://isvara-krsna/sankhya-karika/skmv_20].

Vyāsa uses paramārthatas to collapse the plurality of observed dharma-, lakṣaṇa-, and avasthā-pariṇāmas into a single pariṇāma of the dharmin [cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/3.13]. At the soteriological limit, paramārthatas names the cessation of adarśana through knowledge and the disclosure of puruṣa as kevala-svarūpa [cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/3.55].

In Sucaritamiśra's Mīmāṃsā treatment, the Buddhist division of saṃvṛtisatya and paramārthasatya is rejected because saṃvṛti, as mithyājñāna, cannot share satyatva with what is opposed to it [cite://sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/satya-dvaya-refutation]. What is avisaṃvādi in vyavahāra and arthakriyākāri is simply real; if an external object functions in practice, it exists paramārthata eva rather than under a second, merely conventional satya [cite://sucaritamisra/sloka-varttika/satya-dvaya-refutation].
primary_loci: Sucaritamiśra, Ślokavārttika commentary, satya-dvaya refutation (file lines 2969-2981)

per_school.definition: Nāgārjuna frames Buddhist teaching through two truths, lokasaṃvṛtisatya and the truth in the ultimate sense [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8]. That ultimate is the emptiness identical with dependent arising, and it is taught only in reliance on vyavahāra while remaining necessary for nirvāṇa [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18; cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10].

Vasubandhu identifies the dharmas' paramārtha with tathatā, and that tathatā is precisely vijñaptimātratā abiding always thus [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/25]. Ultimate truth here is therefore articulated as suchness rather than as a hidden substance, namely the perfected mode disclosed when dual construction is no longer projected onto cognition [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/25].

Kashmir Śaiva sources identify the pāramārthika with universal caitanya and with Maheśvara himself: consciousness is the world's true nature, and Maheśvara alone is paramārtha [cite://ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/1.1.10-11; cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/mahesvara-eva-paramartha]. The term therefore marks the self-luminous, vimarśa-saturated Śiva-consciousness that grounds all manifestation rather than a second-order truth alongside it [cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/mahesvara-eva-paramartha].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: No on-disk grammatical primary is available here, and the present corpus does not treat paramārtha-satya as a Pāṇinian-Vaiyākaraṇa technical category.

translator_note: 'Ultimate truth' is serviceable, but avoid hearing 'ultimate' as a highest thing. In Buddhist usage the term often names the truth reached when reification drops away, not a supersensible entity. Also avoid easy equivalence with Advaita *pāramārthika* or Theravāda *paramattha-sacca*: the English is shared, the doctrinal content is not.
